title: Chokin' and Tokin'

jokes.text: Student theorizes the cafeteria recycles the same meat through the week — Salisbury steak becomes hamburgers, then meatball heroes, then meatloaf
jokes.setup: Student notices a pattern in the weekly lunch rotation
jokes.punchline: He concludes it must all be the same meat being reformed and re-presented each day
jokes.explanation: The escalating meat-lifecycle theory is genuinely funny observational logic — the kind of paranoid cafeteria reasoning every school kid has had. It builds nicely across multiple beats.

jokes.text: After the whole meat-recycling debate, a different student steps up and says 'I'll have the Salisbury steak, please' — worker responds 'Excellent choice, sir'
jokes.setup: A debate about whether the meat is recycled garbage has just concluded
jokes.punchline: Someone orders it anyway, and the worker treats it as a dignified fine-dining moment
jokes.explanation: A tight tag joke that lands because of perfect structure: the debate sets up absurdity, and the mock-formal 'Excellent choice, sir' punctures it with deadpan elegance. Brief, well-earned.

jokes.text: Daniel calls the cafeteria food 'Salisbury dog meat' while ordering
jokes.setup: Ordering lunch like a normal person
jokes.punchline: Daniel casually brands it dog meat without breaking stride
jokes.explanation: Minor callback that's character-appropriate for Daniel's laconic cynicism but doesn't add much beyond an extra beat on the same joke.

jokes.text: Nick says 'I could eat again, I'm kinda hungry' — Lindsay says 'You're kinda stoned' — Nick: 'I'm very stoned'
jokes.setup: Nick offers to go back to Denny's even though he's already eating school lunch
jokes.punchline: His explanation for his appetite is simply that he's 'very stoned' — a correction upward from Lindsay's 'kinda'
jokes.explanation: The correction from 'kinda' to 'very' is the joke — Nick's complete lack of self-consciousness about being stoned at school lunch is perfectly character-integrated. The economy is sharp. The escalation within a single exchange is well-constructed.

jokes.text: After Lindsay storms off upset, Daniel says 'What's wrong with Lindsay?' — Nick says 'I don't know, man. She's crazy.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is visibly upset about Nick being stoned again
jokes.punchline: Nick, who is clearly the source of the conflict, diagnoses Lindsay as the crazy one
jokes.explanation: Classic stoner obliviousness. Nick's total lack of self-awareness is the joke, and it's delivered efficiently. The irony is clear without being spelled out.

jokes.text: Nick pressures dealer Mark about being dry; Mark says his dad found his grow lights and ran over them with his car
jokes.setup: Nick accuses Mark of lying about being dry, says he knows Mark grows his own
jokes.punchline: Mark admits he did grow his own, but his dad found the grow lights and literally ran them over with his car
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'ran over them with his car' is what makes it work — not just confiscated, but physically destroyed by vehicular force. Grounds the drug subplot in recognizable suburban-parent-rage comedy.

jokes.text: Beat after the grow-lights story where Nick's dad says 'He better have' — implying approval of the car-running-over
jokes.setup: Mark explains his dad destroyed his grow lights
jokes.punchline: An adult responds with a dry 'He better have' — the entire situation is normalized as appropriate parenting
jokes.explanation: Quick tag with efficient economy. The deadpan approval is mildly amusing but not particularly distinctive.

jokes.text: Nick tells Mark 'The customer is king, Mark. The customer is king.'
jokes.setup: Nick is frustrated the dealer has no product
jokes.punchline: Nick invokes formal customer-service rhetoric to a teenage drug dealer as if filing a complaint with a manager
jokes.explanation: The incongruity of corporate customer-service language applied to a high school drug transaction is genuinely funny. Efficient and character-specific to Nick's oblivious self-seriousness.

jokes.text: Harold flipping through TV channels, sighing — 'Pelicans. Try 2. No, no. Try 4.' [Sigh] '7.' — settling with 'Ah, that's it.'
jokes.setup: Dad searches for something to watch on TV
jokes.punchline: The laborious channel-by-channel search, punctuated by a sigh, lands on something he's satisfied with only after exhausting the options — the joke is in the resigned domesticity
jokes.explanation: A gentle visual/behavioral comedy beat establishing Harold as a lovably exasperated suburban dad. Not a big laugh but an effective character moment — the sigh before '7' is the key beat.

jokes.text: Harold sarcastically tells Lindsay the Johnsons should pay her 'a king's ransom to lay around drinking pop and watching TV on their couch'
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains that $1.50/hour babysitting is slave wages
jokes.punchline: Harold's sarcastic description of babysitting duties — 'lay around drinking pop and watching TV' — deflates her complaint by being largely accurate
jokes.explanation: Harold's dadly sarcasm is perfectly in character. The joke works because the description of babysitting IS basically accurate, which makes Lindsay's complaint hollow. Good parental comedy.

jokes.text: Sam says his favorite Charlie's Angels character is Bosley — Harold is baffled: 'Nobody watches this show for Bosley'
jokes.setup: The family is watching Charlie's Angels; Harold asks Sam who his favorite Angel is
jokes.punchline: Sam names the frumpy, decidedly un-sexy male assistant Bosley rather than any of the beautiful women
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Sam. His earnest defense of Bosley ('He's funny') captures his total obliviousness to the sexual subtext of the show everyone else is watching it for. The Harold reaction sells it — 'Nobody watches this show for Bosley' is the ideal straight-man line. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Harold is wounded that Sam doesn't share his appreciation for Kate Jackson specifically
jokes.setup: Sam defends Bosley as funny
jokes.punchline: Harold's response is 'Yeah, well, he's no Kate Jackson' — revealing Harold's own very specific Angel preference
jokes.explanation: The joke pivots from Sam's obliviousness to Harold's very-much-present awareness of the show's appeal. 'He's no Kate Jackson' is the kind of specific period detail that makes Freaks and Geeks feel genuine. Harold's semi-defensive delivery implies he's been watching this show for reasons he doesn't want to examine too closely.

jokes.text: Harold tells Lindsay she can 'test the waters' too — after she gets married
jokes.setup: Harold encourages Sam to start dating; Lindsay asks why she doesn't get the same encouragement
jokes.punchline: Harold casually says she can date after she's married — a 1980s-dad double standard delivered without irony
jokes.explanation: Harold's completely earnest double standard — women date after marriage — is a perfect period-specific dad joke. No irony from Harold, which makes it funnier. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Harold tells Sam 'The Cindy ship has sailed. It's time to board another vessel.' — Sam responds: 'Another what?'
jokes.setup: Harold tries to give Sam a nautical-metaphor pep talk about moving on
jokes.punchline: Sam doesn't register the metaphor at all — 'Another what?' — completely missing it
jokes.explanation: Sam's literal-minded deflation of Harold's earnest metaphor is very true to the character. The joke is in the total non-connection between father and son, which is both funny and a little sad — classic Freaks and Geeks tone.

jokes.text: Neal rhapsodizes over a 'New Tri-Folder Organizer' with genuine excitement: 'MM! Very impressive. I have one of those babies myself.'
jokes.setup: Sam shows off a new binder
jokes.punchline: Neal treats a school organizer like a luxury consumer item, expressing connoisseur-level enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Neal — the kind of kid who would have strong binder opinions. The adult register ('I have one of those babies myself') applied to school supplies is a small but effective character joke.

jokes.text: Neal suggests Sam target Vicki instead of Cindy — 'The Cindy Sanders of this world are just... hors d'oeuvres. Vicki is a 5-course meal.' Sam: 'Hor-what?'
jokes.setup: Neal delivers a sophisticated food metaphor about women as romantic options
jokes.punchline: Sam has no idea what 'hors d'oeuvres' means, completely deflating Neal's romantic wisdom
jokes.explanation: A great double-character joke: Neal's absurdly sophisticated French food metaphor for teenage romance is funny on its own, but Sam's complete blankness — 'Hor-what?' — is the perfect punchline. Both characters are entirely themselves. The deflation of Neal's worldliness is swift and well-constructed.

jokes.text: Kid offers Bill 'crushed nuts with your sundae' — Bill's confused 'My what?' — then gets hit with the dessert / physical gag
jokes.setup: Bully uses the double-entendre 'crushed nuts' to set up a humiliation gag
jokes.punchline: Bill's oblivious 'My what?' followed by the gag being executed physically
jokes.explanation: Weak schoolyard double-entendre — the oldest trick in the bully handbook. Bill's genuine confusion rescues it slightly (very on-brand), but the joke itself is lazy. Sam's follow-up 'That guy needs a new joke' is a meta-acknowledgment that the show knows it's a tired bit.

jokes.text: Sam deadpans: 'That guy needs a new joke' immediately after the crushed-nuts gag
jokes.setup: An ancient and predictable bully joke has just been executed
jokes.punchline: Sam's weary critical assessment — treating the bully like a failing comedian — is more sophisticated than the gag it's commenting on
jokes.explanation: The meta-commentary earns its laugh by being smarter than the joke it's critiquing. Sam's dry critical distance from his own humiliation is unexpectedly funny and character-appropriate. Six words doing a lot of work.

jokes.text: After Miss Foote sneezes, Bill says 'God bless—whoa. God bless you, Miss Foote' — the 'whoa' implying a powerful sneeze he was not prepared for
jokes.setup: Teacher sneezes; Bill begins the automatic social reflex of saying 'God bless you'
jokes.punchline: The sneeze apparently interrupts his response mid-sentence — the 'whoa' suggests it was an impressively violent sneeze
jokes.explanation: Small but charming — the 'whoa' mid-blessing is a subtle physical comedy beat. Bill's sincerity makes it sweet as well as funny.

jokes.text: Bill offers to close the window for Miss Foote; another student whispers 'Brown noser' — class laughs
jokes.setup: Bill helpfully offers to close the window for the sneezing teacher
jokes.punchline: Alan's whispered 'Brown noser' shames Bill for the act of basic human kindness
jokes.explanation: Functional classroom cringe — the kind of moment that establishes social dynamics. Not clever, but true to the environment. The class laughing at Bill's expense is more uncomfortable than funny.

jokes.text: Bill lists his allergies: 'bees and air and cats and dogs. Well, not dogs. Well, some dogs.'
jokes.setup: Teacher asks what Bill is allergic to
jokes.punchline: 'Air' buried in a list of normal allergens, then the self-correction on dogs that reveals he's thought about this a great deal
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Bees and air' is the unexpected escalation — being allergic to air itself — then the dog caveat ('Well, not dogs. Well, some dogs.') adds a second layer of comedy through Bill's earnest precision about his own condition. Deeply character-specific: only Bill would be allergic to air AND need to clarify the dog situation. Quotable and genuinely original.

jokes.text: Bill matter-of-factly: 'If I eat a peanut, I could die.' Teacher: 'Wow. Really?' Bill: 'Yeah. It happened once.'
jokes.setup: Bill concludes his allergy list with the peanut revelation
jokes.punchline: The casual confirmation 'Yeah. It happened once' — as if nearly dying is an ordinary biographical fact
jokes.explanation: Bill's total equanimity about having nearly died is the joke. 'It happened once' is a masterclass in deadpan understatement — the horror is in the casualness. This line also sets up the entire episode's B-plot, so it's structurally load-bearing.

jokes.text: Bully: 'So don't eat 'em, dork.' Bill: 'I don't. If I did, I could die.'
jokes.setup: Bully offers the 'helpful' advice not to eat peanuts
jokes.punchline: Bill's completely literal response — explaining that not eating them is precisely how he stays alive — takes the insult at face value and wins by refusing to be wounded
jokes.explanation: Bill's literal-minded earnestness disarms the bully's cruelty without him even trying. His dignified deadpan in the face of mockery is a running character comedy element.

jokes.text: Bill outs Neal's psoriasis as 'lizard skin' and Sam's blood-fainting in class — completely torpedoing any social progress
jokes.setup: Bill is trying to say that many people have ailments like his
jokes.punchline: In doing so, he publicly broadcasts his friends' most embarrassing conditions to the whole class in graphic detail
jokes.explanation: The situation is perfectly constructed: Bill is trying to normalize his own condition through solidarity, but in doing so demolishes any social standing his friends have. His obliviousness is total. The class laughing at each revelation is a perfect cringe escalation. Very true to the show's sensibility.

jokes.text: Sam confronts Bill: 'I got a better chance with Miss Foote than you have with Vicki, and I don't have a chance at all.'
jokes.setup: Sam and Bill argue about who's damaged whose social standing more; Bill claims he's not flirting with Miss Foote
jokes.punchline: Bill's self-assessment is ruthlessly accurate — he acknowledges he has zero chance with Miss Foote, which makes his higher probability relative to Sam's Vicki chances darkly funny
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent — the logic builds: Bill compares himself favorably to Sam's chances with Vicki, but his anchor point is zero probability with a teacher. The layers of self-awareness and self-deprecation land simultaneously. This is economical, character-specific, and genuinely funny. One of the episode's strongest lines.

jokes.text: Nick, who is clearly high, asks the freaks for pot. Kim: 'Um, not be stoned?'
jokes.setup: Nick is frantically asking everyone for weed, saying he doesn't know what he'll do without it
jokes.punchline: Kim offers the obvious, unthinkable alternative: just don't be stoned
jokes.explanation: The deadpan suggestion of sobriety as a solution lands because Nick has completely ruled it out — Kim's dry efficiency exposes his dependency without moralizing. Economy is the main strength.

jokes.text: Daniel accuses Nick of never passing a joint in his life; Nick's wounded 'You haven't passed a joint in your life'
jokes.setup: Daniel calls out Nick for being a cheap bastard who never shares weed
jokes.punchline: Nick deflects with the same accusation back at Daniel — both are almost certainly true, making neither claim an actual defense
jokes.explanation: Mild situational comedy about stoner economics. The mutual accusation is funny in its honesty but doesn't rise above functional.

jokes.text: Ken suggests that instead of throwing away the weed, Daniel should 'donate it to a worthy cause. Like, uh, me.'
jokes.setup: Daniel announces he's going to throw away his weed to 'stay sharp'
jokes.punchline: Ken reframes his request for the weed as charitable giving
jokes.explanation: Ken's philanthropic framing of his selfish request is efficient and in-character. Not exceptional but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: Daniel refuses to give Nick the weed: 'I can't give it to you.' / Nick: 'Why not?' / Daniel: 'I care about you.'
jokes.setup: Nick is begging for Daniel's discarded weed. Daniel, who is also a heavy user and barely reformed, refuses.
jokes.punchline: 'I care about you.' — Daniel's sudden sobriety posture, immediately hypocritical given everything.
jokes.explanation: The irony is thick — Daniel, chronic user, deploying the language of genuine concern while refusing to pass his own stash. The straight-faced 'I care about you' is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Nick after 5 days without pot: 'I almost smoked my mom's ferns last night.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks how Nick is holding up without weed
jokes.punchline: Nick reveals his withdrawal desperation has reached the point of eyeing houseplants as potential substitutes
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'I almost smoked my mom's ferns' is specific, absurd, and entirely believable coming from Nick. The image is both hilarious and gently devastating — it captures the pathetic quality of his dependency without being heavy-handed. Quotable, original, and perfectly character-integrated. Approaches the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: Lindsay notes Nick's eyes are clear for once; Nick: 'At least I'm saving money on eye drops'
jokes.setup: Lindsay observes that Nick looks good — his eyes are clear
jokes.punchline: Nick identifies the one silver lining of sobriety as eye drop savings
jokes.explanation: A small, well-constructed joke that reveals Nick's normal state requires Visine maintenance. The practical framing of sobriety as a financial benefit is nicely deadpan.

jokes.text: During a history lesson about George Washington Carver and his work with peanuts, teacher adds: 'And for being Bill's mortal enemy' — then self-explains: 'Because you're allergic to peanuts'
jokes.setup: History lesson about George Washington Carver's peanut innovations
jokes.punchline: Teacher's aside connecting the lesson to Bill's peanut allergy, then immediately explaining the joke she just made
jokes.explanation: The joke is actually funnier because she immediately explains it — 'Because you're allergic to peanuts' — which is exactly how an awkward, well-meaning teacher would handle a classroom joke. The self-explanation undermines her attempt at wit, which is itself funny.

jokes.text: Bill tells Miss Foote: 'I just don't like you talking so much about my allergies. It makes me look like a geek.'
jokes.setup: Bill privately addresses his discomfort with the teacher's attention to his allergies
jokes.punchline: Bill's earnest concern about his geek reputation — delivered by the most visibly geeky character on the show, to his teacher, about his deadly food allergies
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is rich — Bill is worried about looking like a geek while embodying geekhood in his every atom, and he's having this image-management conversation with his teacher. Sweet and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: Miss Foote: 'You know, Bill, you're not a geek.' Bill: 'Tell that to the rest of the world.'
jokes.setup: Teacher tries to reassure Bill about his social standing
jokes.punchline: Bill's weary, world-worn dismissal — 'Tell that to the rest of the world' — from a 14-year-old
jokes.explanation: Bill's deadpan self-awareness is perfectly calibrated — he knows exactly what he is, and he's not bitter about it, just realistic. The phrase 'Tell that to the rest of the world' carries a weight well beyond its setup. One of Bill's finest character moments.

jokes.text: Gordon declares he doesn't just like sci-fi, he 'LOVE, LOVE, LOVES it!' and immediately asks whose mom is driving
jokes.setup: Sam and Neal awkwardly try to offload the sci-fi convention on Gordon so they can go to the cheerleading competition
jokes.punchline: Gordon's explosive enthusiasm for sci-fi is so outsized and immediate that it's almost alarming, then he pivots directly to logistics
jokes.explanation: Gordon's over-the-top enthusiasm is a good character beat. The triple 'love' is performative, but the immediate pivot to 'whose mom drives' is a nice deflation.

jokes.text: Gordon: 'Hey, maybe my mom should drive because she puts all my make-up on in the parking lot before I go in.'
jokes.setup: Deciding logistics for the sci-fi convention
jokes.punchline: Gordon casually mentions he wears make-up to the sci-fi convention, and that his mom applies it in the parking lot — implying it's a costume thing he's comfortable with
jokes.explanation: A quietly excellent character detail — Gordon's mom doing his sci-fi convention make-up in the parking lot is both specific and absurd. The casual delivery makes it funnier. This is the kind of small detail Freaks and Geeks does better than almost any other show.

jokes.text: Basketball scene: player dunks, someone says 'that's easy for me... and Kareem Abdul-Jabbar' — visual comedy of presumably non-Kareem-level player
jokes.setup: Someone makes a difficult basketball shot look easy
jokes.punchline: The Kareem comparison implies either overconfidence or self-aware absurdity
jokes.explanation: A throwaway sports brag joke with period-appropriate NBA reference. Weak without more context — can't tell if it's self-aware or earnest, which limits the comedy.

jokes.text: Mark returns with weed saying 'The eagle has landed' — then announces a 'harvest sale'
jokes.setup: Earlier drought of weed; Mark said he'd find some
jokes.punchline: Mark applies the gravitas of the moon landing to drug delivery, then the sales language 'harvest sale' to a transaction with a teenager
jokes.explanation: Mild callback with the 'eagle has landed' euphemism landing adequately. The drought/flood metaphor that follows is slightly clever but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Mark tells Nick the weed is 'hydroponic' — Nick: 'Like the lettuce?' Mark: 'Yeah, like the lettuce.'
jokes.setup: Mark vouches for the quality of the weed by calling it hydroponic
jokes.punchline: Nick's frame of reference for 'hydroponic' is supermarket lettuce — which is correct but misses the implied prestige entirely
jokes.explanation: A small gem. Nick reduces premium cannabis terminology to grocery store produce, and Mark's flat 'Yeah, like the lettuce' confirms he can't argue with the accuracy. The exchange is perfectly economical and very quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay declines to smoke weed: 'Dog crap is here, and we don't smoke that.'
jokes.setup: Nick argues weed is natural — 'It's from the earth. Why would it be there if we weren't supposed to smoke it?'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's counter-example destroys the naturalistic fallacy argument instantly with dog feces
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. Lindsay demolishes the 'it's natural therefore good' argument with an airtight, grotesque counterexample. The logic is bulletproof, the image is funny, and the delivery is sharp. It's both a great debate response AND perfectly in character for the girl drifting toward the freaks while still having her academic brain engaged. Highly quotable. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor and arguably above it in terms of originality and economy.

jokes.text: After being built up as a transcendent experience, the first pot-smoking session is shown as: Nick enthusiastically listening to drum fills while Lindsay sits there bored and unmoved
jokes.setup: Nick has been hyping marijuana as a profound, fun experience
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's first high amounts to sitting on a couch while Nick loses his mind over a drum fill
jokes.explanation: A sustained visual/behavioral comedy beat that earns its laughs through subverted expectations. The anti-climax IS the joke. Nick's ecstasy over the drum fill while Lindsay experiences nothing is a beautiful deflation of the promised drug experience. Character integration is excellent — Nick becomes the cautionary tale Lindsay suspected.

jokes.text: Nick interrupts the relationship argument to say: 'Oh, shhh... listen to this drum fill. OHH! [laughs] That guy is AMAZING. Do you know how hard that is?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is in the middle of making a serious point about Nick's pot addiction
jokes.punchline: Nick completely drops the conversation to appreciate a drum fill with intense, genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect character comedy moment. Nick's stoned rapture over the drum fill arrives exactly when the scene needs dramatic weight, making it both funnier and more devastating. The 'That guy is AMAZING' is delivered with such sincerity that it transcends the joke into something almost moving. High character integration — this moment becomes definitive Nick. Matches the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Nick to Lindsay: 'You're a drag. And you know what? Here, you see it? I'm really addicted. How am I gonna live without it?' — then dramatically throws the bag away
jokes.setup: Nick and Lindsay argue about whether he's addicted
jokes.punchline: Nick's sarcastic performance of throwing away the weed to 'prove' he's not addicted — a gesture that actually confirms he is
jokes.explanation: Nick's petulant sarcasm — 'I'm really addicted, how am I gonna live without it?' — as he throws away the weed is funny precisely because the overdramatic performance proves Lindsay's point. The dramatic irony is clear and well-timed.

jokes.text: Bill spotting Mr. Peanut mascot in a magazine: 'He's probably eating a peanut butter and jelly sandwich, the liar.'
jokes.setup: Bill and Sam are passing time in the school corridor; Bill spots the Mr. Peanut mascot
jokes.punchline: Bill is personally offended by the anthropomorphic legume's implied self-cannibalism and considers it deceptive
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best moments. Bill's paranoid logic — Mr. Peanut is 'a liar' for potentially consuming his own kind — is absurdist comedy at its most specific and earned. It only works coming from someone with a life-threatening peanut allergy who has clearly spent time thinking about peanut mythology. The word 'liar' is doing spectacular work. Highly quotable, extremely original, and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Hmm. Let's find out if he's lying.' — implying they're going to investigate whether Mr. Peanut is eating peanuts
jokes.setup: Bill accuses Mr. Peanut of being a liar
jokes.punchline: Sam immediately adopts Bill's logic and treats the mascot-cannibalism question as an investigable hypothesis
jokes.explanation: Sam's immediate buy-in to the absurdist premise deepens the joke. His serious 'Let's find out if he's lying' treats the mascot as a real suspect, which extends the joke beautifully.

jokes.text: Bill's 'I'm sorry' to Gordon about skipping the convention; Gordon: 'I was gonna lend you my Yoda mask, too.'
jokes.setup: Bill apologizes for bailing on the sci-fi convention
jokes.punchline: Gordon mentions the Yoda mask as the specific grievance — the mask lending was apparently a significant commitment
jokes.explanation: A small character beat — Gordon's sci-fi commitment is quantified by the lending of a Yoda mask. Mild but warm.

jokes.text: Gordon to Bill: 'You are, but we're too hungry to care.'
jokes.setup: Bill self-consciously wonders if his friends think he's too geeky to eat with
jokes.punchline: Gordon confirms he is, in fact, too geeky, but hunger overrides social concerns
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed. Gordon doesn't offer false comfort — he agrees Bill is too geeky — but then overrides that assessment with hunger pragmatism. The honesty is both funny and oddly sweet. The line is extremely economical and has a lovely rhythm. One of the episode's best one-liners.

jokes.text: Bill bites his sandwich and notes it tastes 'peanuty crunchy' — with increasing alarm
jokes.setup: The audience knows (or strongly suspects) someone put peanuts in Bill's sandwich
jokes.punchline: Bill's gradual recognition — 'crunchy... like peanuty crunchy' — is comedy of dread, where we watch someone walk toward something terrible in real-time
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is dark and structural — it's funny precisely because the audience has been given all the information. 'Peanuty crunchy' is a perfect Bill word choice, casual and scientific. The scene works both as comedy (dramatic irony) and drama (genuine horror). This is the show operating at its most sophisticated.

jokes.text: Bill realizes his sandwich tastes 'peanuty crunchy' — the horrified realization landing across his face as Sam and Neal watch
jokes.setup: Bill has already eaten from his sandwich; something seems off about the texture
jokes.punchline: 'Like... like peanuty crunchy' — the audience understands the stakes before Bill fully does
jokes.explanation: The horror-comedy timing is excellent — the audience has been primed with the allergy information for the entire episode, and 'peanuty crunchy' is the moment of tragic realization. It's funny and terrifying simultaneously, which is peak Freaks and Geeks.

jokes.text: Sam asks Bill if he wants him to get Miss Foote to discuss the sandwich — a callback to the Bill/Miss Foote flirtation joke
jokes.setup: Bill seems to be going into anaphylactic shock
jokes.punchline: Sam makes a joke about Bill's alleged flirting with Miss Foote — tone-deaf to the emergency
jokes.explanation: The callback lands here because the timing is darkly absurd — Sam uses Bill's possible medical emergency to make a joke about his crush. The juxtaposition of a potentially fatal situation with the petty flirting subplot is funny.

jokes.text: Bully Alan appears and asks 'Hey Haverchuck, what happens now? Should we call an ambulance?' in a mocking tone — then immediately the answer is yes
jokes.setup: Bill is beginning to react to the peanut; Alan is taunting him
jokes.punchline: Alan's sarcastic ambulance question is immediately answered affirmatively — he accidentally asked the right question in the worst way
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective — Alan's mock question 'should we call an ambulance?' gets answered 'Yes' in reality. The joke is in the collapse between Alan's intended mockery and the actual situation. This also begins Alan's character arc turn for the episode.

jokes.text: Neal tells Mrs. Haverchuck: 'Is he gonna die?' — she reassures him no. Then Sam follows up: 'Could you not use the word die in a hospital?'
jokes.setup: Sam asks if Bill is going to die; Mrs. Haverchuck asks him not to use that word in a hospital
jokes.punchline: Sam points out — correctly — that she just asked if he was 'still alive,' which is essentially the same question
jokes.explanation: Sam's logical observation — that 'is he alive' and 'will he die' are equivalent questions — is both funny and correct. The follow-up 'That's an optimistic question. Alive is good. Die is bad. Trust me on this. My dad's a dentist' adds a perfect extra layer of absurdist logic.

jokes.text: Sam explains his semantic distinction: 'Alive is good. Die is bad. Trust me on this. My dad's a dentist.'
jokes.setup: Sam defends his word choice vs. Mrs. Haverchuck's objection
jokes.punchline: Sam invokes his dentist father's authority as the credential for his philosophical position on the words 'alive' and 'die'
jokes.explanation: A top-tier joke in the episode. 'Trust me on this. My dad's a dentist.' as the authority for a life/death semantic argument is completely surreal logic — dentists have almost nothing to do with this — but delivered with Sam's complete earnestness, it's hilarious. The non-sequitur credential is absurdist perfection. Economy is outstanding. Highly quotable. Comparable to or above the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Frank (former student) on returning to school: 'Girls are cuter. A lot cuter.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Russo introduces a former student who has come back to visit. Frank reflects on how things have changed.
jokes.punchline: Frank's primary observation about his alma mater: the girls got prettier since he graduated.
jokes.explanation: Establishing Frank's character quickly — the burnt-out former student for whom time has not been kind. The observation is mildly creepy which is part of the point. Sets up the 'this is your future' moral lesson Mr. Russo intends.

jokes.text: Frank claims he saw Pink Floyd at the Pyramids — Ken gently notes Floyd has never played the pyramids. Frank: 'Oh yeah. You're right. It was the Dead. I just got confused because I was thinking of another guy I went to both shows with.'
jokes.setup: Frank casually drops that he saw Pink Floyd at the Egyptian Pyramids
jokes.punchline: When corrected, Frank smoothly transitions to the Dead but his explanation — 'thinking of another guy I went to both shows with' — doesn't actually make sense
jokes.explanation: Frank's correction-and-pivot is a brilliant portrait of a scrambled brain — he smoothly accepts the correction but his reasoning for the confusion makes no logical sense (the man, not the band, is what links the two shows?). The confidence with which he delivers nonsense is the joke. Feig/Apatow at their best: funny and a little devastating.

jokes.text: Frank: 'Hey, saw Floyd at the Laser Dome. They weren't there, but it was their music.'
jokes.setup: Frank is trying to establish his Pink Floyd credentials
jokes.punchline: Frank volunteers that he saw Floyd at the Laser Dome — then immediately clarifies the band wasn't present, just their recorded music
jokes.explanation: The episode's strongest single line for pure absurdist construction. 'They weren't there, but it was their music' is simultaneously true of every laser dome show ever and yet a completely delusional credential. Frank has entirely lost the ability to distinguish attendance from proximity to a recording. The economy — ten words, perfect delivery — is outstanding. Comparable to or exceeding the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Mr. Russo tells Daniel and Ken: 'Keep burning the bong at both ends and you'll wind up just like him.'
jokes.setup: Russo is using Frank as a morality lesson
jokes.punchline: The mangled idiom 'burning the bong at both ends' — combining 'burning the candle at both ends' with bong imagery
jokes.explanation: A small but clever wordplay moment — Russo's drug-adapted idiom reveals he's more tuned into the culture than he lets on. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier than if it were played for laughs.

jokes.text: Russo says 'How dumb do I look?' — Ken: 'Do you really want to know?'
jokes.setup: Russo expresses skepticism about Daniel's excuse that he was trying to get rid of his weed
jokes.punchline: Ken's 'Do you really want to know?' to a rhetorical question — answering a figure of speech literally and dangerously
jokes.explanation: Ken's deadpan willingness to answer a rhetorical question is perfectly in character — his dry, fearless irreverence in the face of authority is one of his defining traits. Good economy.

jokes.text: Russo: 'Learn from Frank... or you'll be Frank. It's your choice.'
jokes.setup: Russo wraps up his anti-drug lecture
jokes.punchline: The rhyming epigram 'Learn from Frank or you'll be Frank' — simultaneously a meaningful threat and an inadvertent pun
jokes.explanation: A crisp closer to the Frank subplot. The rhyming epigram is the kind of self-satisfied adult advice that actually lands, and the fact that the counselor seems pleased with his own construction makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ken to Russo: 'As much as that's possible.'
jokes.setup: Russo asks 'Do we understand each other?'
jokes.punchline: Ken's qualifier 'As much as that's possible' refuses to fully concede mutual understanding while technically agreeing
jokes.explanation: Ken's perfectly calibrated insubordination — agreeing just barely enough to avoid consequences while still refusing to fully capitulate. The economy is excellent; this tells you everything about Ken in six words.

jokes.text: Sam and Neal comfort themselves in the hospital waiting room by planning what kind of ghost Bill would be: 'Maybe he'd be like Casper the Friendly Ghost'
jokes.setup: Bill is in critical condition; Sam and Neal are sitting in the hospital waiting room
jokes.punchline: Instead of processing grief, they immediately pivot to speculating about Bill's afterlife social dynamics
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific — two teenage boys dealing with a possible friend's death by immediately genre-filing him as a Casper-type. The coping mechanism reveals exactly who Sam and Neal are. The comedy and the pathos work together seamlessly.

jokes.text: Sam suddenly: 'But it wouldn't be like that, would it? He'd just be dead and gone forever, wouldn't he?'
jokes.setup: Sam and Neal have been cheerfully planning Bill's ghost logistics
jokes.punchline: Sam's sudden return to reality — 'He'd just be dead and gone forever' — collapses the fantasy and creates an uncomfortable emotional beat
jokes.explanation: The emotional pivot is the joke — the comedy of the ghost-planning collapses into genuine fear. This beat works as dark comedy and as drama, and the 'Yeah' response is the perfect deflated punchline to the whole sequence. Classic Freaks and Geeks tonal mastery.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'What do people do when they're not stoned? I don't know. Relate to one another?'
jokes.setup: Daniel is sober (or trying to be) and genuinely doesn't know how to fill the time
jokes.punchline: His best guess for sober human activity is 'relating to one another' — delivered as if this is a deeply foreign concept
jokes.explanation: One of Daniel's best moments. His genuine bewilderment at the concept of sober human connection — offered tentatively as a hypothesis — reveals how thoroughly weed has mediated his social life. Economical and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Lindsay tells her dad she can't babysit because of 'woman problems' — Harold immediately: 'I don't want to hear about it. Just get over there.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay (stoned, trying to get out of babysitting) invokes 'woman problems' to deflect her dad
jokes.punchline: Harold's immediate, panicked retreat from the topic — 'I don't want to hear about it' — is classic male discomfort, while also accidentally working as a trump card for Lindsay
jokes.explanation: Harold's panicked retreat is very in character — the same man who said Lindsay can date after marriage is predictably useless when confronted with female biology. The gag is familiar but well-executed.

jokes.text: Millie sees stoned Lindsay and says flatly: 'You're on the pot.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay shows up to Millie's door asking for help, claiming it'll be 'fun'
jokes.punchline: Millie's immediate, clipped diagnosis — 'You're on the pot' — delivered with zero drama, as if noting a hair out of place
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'on the pot' is doing enormous work — it's exactly how an evangelical good-girl in 1980 would say it. The archaic drug terminology, Millie's complete matter-of-factness, and the contrast with Lindsay's attempt at casual normality make this a standout character comedy line. Very Millie, very Freaks and Geeks.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'How could you tell?' Millie: 'I'm not dumb, Lindsay. I know what high people look like. I went to a Seals and Crofts concert last summer.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is amazed Millie can tell she's high
jokes.punchline: Millie cites her Seals and Crofts concert attendance as her drug-identification credential — a gentle Christian duo not typically associated with drug culture
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best jokes in the series. Millie's credential for recognizing drug use — a Seals and Crofts concert — is perfectly absurd because (a) Seals and Crofts were clean-living Bahá'í musicians, (b) Millie would only attend a safe Christian-adjacent concert, and (c) the fact that even a Seals and Crofts show had high people reveals that drug culture was absolutely everywhere. The specificity of 'Seals and Crofts' is genius. No other character on this show could deliver this line. Maximum character integration, economy, and originality. Comfortably above the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Millie agrees to come help only 'for the safety of the child' — Lindsay thanks her; Millie: 'No, thank your dealer.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is grateful Millie agreed to come help
jokes.punchline: Millie redirects the gratitude to Lindsay's drug supplier as the true agent of the situation
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed kicker. Millie's 'No, thank your dealer' reframes the entire social situation — she's not being nice, she's responding to a situation someone else created. The word 'dealer' in Millie's mouth is itself a joke. Highly quotable, excellent economy, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Millie: 'Thank your dealer.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay thanks Millie for coming.
jokes.punchline: 'Thank your dealer.' Millie's caustic redirect — she's not taking the credit, she's placing the blame.
jokes.explanation: Three words, nine syllables, a complete worldview. 'Thank your dealer' from Millie — the Christian good girl — is perfectly jarring. It acknowledges the situation without moralizing further, assigns causality correctly, and is delivered with such economy that it lands like a punch. This is the kind of line that gets quoted. High character integration: only Millie can say this with that particular mix of judgment and pragmatism. This reaches and possibly slightly exceeds the 8.0 anchor standard.

jokes.text: The Johnson parents' frantic babysitting instructions — knives, dog-riding, poison control, balloons
jokes.setup: Parents are leaving Lindsay and Millie with their child. They give instructions.
jokes.punchline: The instructions escalate from normal (phone numbers on the fridge) to specific ('Don't let him ride the dog') — each new rule implying an increasingly catastrophic previous incident.
jokes.explanation: The escalating parental anxiety is a reliable comedy structure. 'Don't let him ride the dog' is the payoff — implying this has happened and it was a problem. The Poison Control detail is solid. Lindsay and Millie being absolutely the wrong people to receive these instructions adds dark irony.

jokes.text: Jean (Sam's mom): 'When I was pregnant with Sam, I didn't think twice about nutrition. I was drinking a lot back then.'
jokes.setup: Jean is empathizing with Mrs. Haverchuck's guilt about Bill's allergies
jokes.punchline: Jean casually reveals she drank heavily during Sam's pregnancy as a parallel confession
jokes.explanation: Jean's casual confession of drinking during pregnancy, offered as solidarity, is darkly funny. The casualness of 'I was drinking a lot back then' is the joke — it's not processed as a confession, just a fact.

jokes.text: Jean caps the confessional escalation: 'When Sam was 4 months old... I dropped him on a brick patio. He had a hairline fracture of the skull.'
jokes.setup: Jean has confessed to drinking and drug use during pregnancy
jokes.punchline: The escalation peaks with dropping infant Sam on a brick patio and causing a skull fracture
jokes.explanation: The final beat of a three-part escalation that builds from bad to worse to jaw-dropping. 'Brick patio' is the specific, terrible detail that makes it land. Sam's awkward 'Thanks, Jean' after a pause is the perfect reaction beat. This whole sequence (jokes 71-73) is among the episode's finest sustained comedy.

jokes.text: Lindsay (high) is found by the kid she's supposed to be babysitting — he's tagged her as 'it' in a game she wasn't playing
jokes.setup: Lindsay is having a mild panic attack while stoned; she's supposed to be in charge
jokes.punchline: A small child has dragooned the incapacitated babysitter into a game of tag against her will
jokes.explanation: The child asserting dominance over the stoned, helpless babysitter is a satisfying comedic reversal. 'Just give me some space, man, OK?' to a small child is Lindsay at peak frazzled.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Nick is stoned all the time. He deals with his parents when he's high. He goes to class when he's high. Why would he do that?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is asking genuine questions about Nick's lifestyle while experiencing her first high
jokes.punchline: The question 'Why would he do that?' — which is the same question she's been asking all episode — finally makes existential sense to her
jokes.explanation: The pivot from judgment to genuine empathy-via-experience is well-constructed. 'Why would he do that?' carries dramatic irony — she now understands why, and the answer is not reassuring.

jokes.text: Lindsay's stoned philosophical spiral: 'What if all of this is a dream? What if it's not even our dream — it's that dog's dream?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Millie are having a conversation about God and meaning
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's drug-fueled philosophy escalates from 'What if it's a dream?' to the dog being the dreamer of reality
jokes.explanation: The absurdist escalation from standard stoner solipsism to 'it's the dog's dream' is earned because it's so specifically Lindsay — her intelligence redirected by THC into increasingly baroque metaphysics. The dog as the dreamer is a funny, specific image.

jokes.text: Millie's response to the dog-dreaming-our-reality theory: 'Life is not that dog's damn dream. We live in God's world.' — then she announces she'll wake the dog to prove it
jokes.setup: Lindsay is convinced they exist only in the dog's dream
jokes.punchline: Millie invokes God's authority against a dog's authority, then decides empirical proof is needed — she will wake the dog
jokes.explanation: Millie's confident theological counter-argument followed by her decision to wake the dog is a perfect comic escalation. The juxtaposition of 'God's world' vs. 'the dog's dream' as competing cosmologies, with Millie treating dog-waking as a viable theological experiment, is hilarious. Only Millie.

jokes.text: Millie wakes the dog: 'ARISE, DOGGY. ARISE.' [dog barks] 'You see? Believe, Lindsay.'
jokes.setup: Millie is proving God's world exists by waking a dog
jokes.punchline: The dog waking up — and Millie treating this as confirmation of God's creation rather than a dog waking up — is her triumphant theological punchline
jokes.explanation: The physical act of Millie declaring 'ARISE, DOGGY. ARISE.' — like a faith healer at a revival — while commanding a dog to prove the existence of God is simply magnificent. 'Believe, Lindsay' as her triumphant coda is the perfect evangelical button. The absurdism, character integration, and escalation all peak here. One of the series' great Millie moments.

jokes.text: Bill, just waking up in hospital: 'Did I almost die?' Mom: 'Yes.' Bill: 'That's cool.'
jokes.setup: Bill wakes up after being in critical condition from anaphylaxis
jokes.punchline: 'That's cool.' — Bill's earnest, immediate assessment of his near-death experience
jokes.explanation: Two words after a life-threatening medical emergency: 'That's cool.' Bill's complete lack of drama about nearly dying — his immediate appraisal being positive — is perfectly him. The economy is maximized: the entire episode's tension is released in two syllables. Deeply character-specific and absurdly funny. One of the best punchlines of the episode.

jokes.text: Bill tells Sam and Neal about Vicki and Maureen crying: 'If you want, you can tell them that I'm still dying' — then 'I mean, if it'll help you guys get a date.'
jokes.setup: Sam tells Bill that the girls were hanging all over them out of worry
jokes.punchline: Bill generously offers to extend his fake-dying status as a gift to his friends' love lives
jokes.explanation: Bill's selfless offer to pretend to still be dying so Sam and Neal can get dates is both hilarious and oddly touching. The follow-up 'I mean, if it'll help you guys get a date' has a beautiful guilelessness — he's done the math and concluded this is a reasonable arrangement. Excellent dark comedy.

jokes.text: Sam: 'You are the king.' Bill accepts this immediately.
jokes.setup: Bill has just offered to fake his ongoing dying for Sam's dating benefit
jokes.punchline: Sam's sincere coronation of Bill — 'You are the king' — is the highest honor Sam can bestow, and Bill accepts it without irony
jokes.explanation: A warm tag to an excellent joke. 'The king' as currency in this specific social economy is funny and sweet.

jokes.text: Lindsay explains why the hospital Jell-O is good: 'Because it was made by food scientists.'
jokes.setup: Nick and Lindsay are eating hospital Jell-O
jokes.punchline: Lindsay attributes the quality to 'food scientists' — a completely deadpan non-explanation that actually explains nothing
jokes.explanation: 'Because it was made by food scientists' is an absurdist tautology delivered with complete conviction. It implies specialized expertise while meaning essentially nothing. The post-high clarity Lindsay has lends it extra funny weight.

jokes.text: Lindsay reminds Millie that they 'used to watch his show. You used to wish you had a pillow stuffed with his hair. Because it was so fluffy.'
jokes.setup: The Mac Davis song plays; Lindsay and Millie reminisce about their shared childhood
jokes.punchline: The specific memory of Millie wanting a pillow stuffed with Mac Davis's hair 'because it was so fluffy' is a surreal childhood memory that implies a very specific fixation
jokes.explanation: The hair-pillow detail is extremely specific and absurd. That Millie specifically wanted his hair made into a pillow — not a poster, not a record — and the justification 'because it was so fluffy' is genuinely funny childhood-innocence comedy. Only these two characters could share this memory.

jokes.text: Millie tells Lindsay: 'Tomorrow, when you're not loaded anymore, you're not gonna believe in God, and you're not gonna want to be my friend anymore.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just declared they'll be best friends again and she believes in God now
jokes.punchline: Millie, with complete clarity, predicts the exact sober reversal that will occur tomorrow — and she's almost certainly right
jokes.explanation: Millie's devastating and accurate forecast is both the funniest and saddest line in the episode. She's not bitter, just clear-eyed. The line is funny because it's so blunt and accurate, and heartbreaking for the same reasons. Perfect Millie character integration — she's smarter than anyone gives her credit for. Among the episode's most affecting moments.

jokes.text: Alan visits Bill in the hospital: 'Hey, Haverchuck.' 'Hey, Alan.' 'I knew you wouldn't die. I can't believe you'd be such a wuss to die from a peanut.'
jokes.setup: Alan comes to apologize/visit Bill in the hospital
jokes.punchline: Alan's non-apology — framing Bill's survival as weakness ('wuss to die from a peanut') — is his idea of reassurance
jokes.explanation: Alan's attempt at human warmth accidentally inverts into an insult: Bill is apparently a coward for having the audacity to survive. The logic is perfectly broken and deeply in character. Alan reaching out while still unable to stop being Alan is excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: Bill: 'Aren't you glad I didn't die?' Alan: 'Yeah, I'm glad for my sake. Think I want to end up in juvie for killing you?'
jokes.setup: Bill gently probes whether Alan cares about him; Alan has been forced into an apology
jokes.punchline: Alan's stated reason for being glad Bill survived is entirely self-interested — he'd go to juvie — not any actual concern for Bill
jokes.explanation: Alan's honest self-interest is funnier than any false warmth would be. 'Think I want to end up in juvie for killing you?' is the exact right Alan answer. But the scene also works because we heard Alan's genuine remorse earlier, making this self-protective bluster funnier and sadder. Good character integration.

tmdb_overview: After fighting with his father, Nick moves out and to Lindsay's surprise begins staying with the Weir's. Cindy tells Bill that she has a crush on Sam. She asks Bill to convince Sam to invite her to a party.
